title: Noncognitive Traits as Fundamental Causes of Health Inequality Baseline Findings from Urban China
abstract: This article evaluates the Heckman hypothesis on the health effects of noncognitive traits in China. The authors also use the robustness of health effects in different contexts to compare the degrees to which socioeconomic status (SES) and adolescent conscientiousness, a major dimension of noncognitive traits, are fundamental causes of health inequality. Drawing on the 2011 Chinese General Social Survey, they provide the first national evidence on the robust benefit of conscientiousness for self-rated health. In contrast, the health effects of SES are contingent: while high achievers of education and income enjoy better health in less developed urban areas, they face health penalties in more developed urban areas. This striking divergence suggests that SES may be less fundamental than adolescent noncognitive traits as causes of health inequality. Moreover, the health benefit of conscientiousness, but not SES, is similar for the Pearl River Delta and Hong Kong, despite their very different institutions and health-care systems.
